local_names.romanized_local: Dahi Vada / Dahi Bhalla / Thayir Vadai

food_subgroup: Lentil fritters soaked in spiced sweetened yogurt

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Dahi Vada. Nearest parent is a lentil-based snack in dairy sauce. A new concept for yogurt-based Indian chaat is recommended.
ontology.foodex2_description: Legume-based fritter in dairy condiment, Indian style (closest parent)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code exists for Dahi Vada; A03SM (legume-based prepared dish) is the closest defensible parent code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Dahi Vada as a specific food item.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Dahi Vada is approximately 200g, comprising two lentil fritters submerged in sweetened yogurt with chutneys and spice toppings.

nutrient_source.title: Dahi Vada — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, Entry EI000020
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods for Dahi Vada (Entry EI000020). Copper, manganese, and vitamin K values are enriched estimates from the same source family with confidence 0.9.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Urad Dal Fritter in Yogurt — University of Sydney GI Database (analogous estimate)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 52 estimated from urad dal fritter in yogurt using the University of Sydney GI Database. Urad dal and yogurt are both established low-GI foods. The soaking process and protein content of the yogurt matrix further moderate the glycemic response. Glycemic load of 19.2 calculated from GI 52 × available carbohydrate 18.5g per 100g × serving size 200g / 100.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 298mg per 100g (596mg per standard 200g serving)
hypertension.guidance: A standard 200g serving contributes approximately 596mg sodium, representing about 26% of the 2300mg daily limit; patients with hypertension should limit chaat masala and tamarind chutney toppings, which are the primary sodium contributors in this dish.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (52) but moderate glycemic load (~19) per serving and 6.5g sugar per 100g from sweetened yogurt
type2_diabetes.guidance: Dahi Vada has a low GI of approximately 52 due to urad dal and yogurt protein, but the sweetened yogurt contributes 6.5g sugar per 100g and the glycemic load per standard serving is approximately 19; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should request unsweetened yogurt and limit portion size to manage postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (1.8g per 100g), zero trans fat, and beneficial legume protein and fibre from urad dal
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Dahi Vada is relatively low in saturated fat (1.8g per 100g) and contains zero trans fat; the urad dal provides plant protein and dietary fibre which support cardiovascular health, though the deep-frying step adds total fat and the sodium content warrants monitoring in patients with concurrent hypertension.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spices and high folate content from urad dal
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Urad dal is an excellent source of folate (38mcg per 100g) and plant protein; yogurt provides probiotics and calcium; cumin and chilli have mild anti-inflammatory properties, making Dahi Vada a generally suitable snack for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dahi Vada is a beloved Indian chaat dish consisting of soft, deep-fried lentil dumplings (vadas) made from ground urad dal (black gram). The preparation involves soaking the fried vadas in water to remove excess oil and achieve a spongy texture, then submerging them in chilled, thick, sweetened plain yogurt (dahi). The dish is finished with generous toppings of dark tamarind chutney (imli chutney), vibrant green mint-coriander chutney, chaat masala, roasted cumin powder, and red chilli powder, often garnished with fresh coriander. Served cold, Dahi Vada delivers a complex flavour profile that is simultaneously creamy, tangy, sweet, and spiced. It is popular across North and South India — known as Dahi Bhalla in North India and Thayir Vadai in Tamil Nadu — and is commonly served as a street food snack, festival food, or restaurant starter. Nutritionally, Dahi Vada provides moderate energy (142 kcal per 100g), meaningful plant protein (5.8g) from urad dal, calcium (95mg) and vitamin B12 (0.22mcg) from yogurt, and folate (38mcg) from the lentil base. The glycemic index is approximately 52 (low), moderated by the protein matrix of yogurt and the resistant starch in urad dal. Sodium is elevated at 298mg per 100g primarily due to chaat masala and chutneys, which is a consideration for hypertensive individuals. The dish is vegetarian, halal, and gluten-free.
common_user_queries: dahi vada vs medu vada calories

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (Entry EI000020), the primary approved source for Indian dishes. Core macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients are well-supported. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using urad dal fritter in yogurt as the reference food, which is the closest defensible match.
notes: Trans fat is reported as 0.0 — confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN source for this preparation. Vitamin D is reported as 0.0 — confirmed zero as this is a plant-and-dairy dish with no significant vitamin D source; yogurt in India is typically not vitamin D fortified. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat values are not reported by the primary source and are set to -1. Water content is not reported by the primary source and is set to -1. Copper (0.12mg), manganese (0.1mg), and vitamin K (1.5mcg) are enriched estimates with confidence 0.9 from the same ICMR-NIN source family. The portions array in the input contained three duplicate entries for '1 serving (200g)'; deduplicated to a single default serving of 200g. GI glycemic load of 19.2 is calculated as: GI 52 × carbohydrate 18.5g per 100g × 200g serving / 100 = 19.24, rounded to 19.2.
